content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem and is worth the usual 100 points. Here, Q=0Q = 0Q=0, i.e. there are no updates to the grid.**

You have a binary grid of size 2×N2 \times N2×N. The jjj-th character of the iii-th row is A(i,j)A_{(i, j)}A(i,j)​.

In one operation, you can choose a row XXX and a column YYY (1≤Y<N1 \le Y < N1≤Y<N); and then swap A(X,Y)A_{(X, Y)}A(X,Y)​ and A(X,Y+1)A_{(X, Y + 1)}A(X,Y+1)​, i.e. you are allowed to make adjacent swaps in the same row.

You want to find out the minimum number of operations needed to make the grid have a **right-down path** from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (2,N)(2, N)(2,N) consisting of **only 1s**, i.e. if you visit (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y) in your path, A(X,Y)A_{(X, Y)}A(X,Y)​ must be 111. If it is impossible, print −1-1−1 instead.

Further, you are given QQQ updates, each of which flips a value in the grid, i.e. you are given a cell (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y) and must change A(X,Y)A_{(X, Y)}A(X,Y)​ to 1−A(X,Y)1 - A_{(X, Y)}1−A(X,Y)​.

After each update, find the new answer of the minimum operations needed for a right-down path from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (2,N)(2, N)(2,N).

The updates are permanent, they don't revert back each time.

**In this version, Q=0Q = 0Q=0 and so there are no updates**.

**Note:** A right-down path is one which moves to either cell (x+1,y)(x + 1, y)(x+1,y) or (x,y+1)(x, y + 1)(x,y+1) from the cell (x,y)(x, y)(x,y).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN and QQQ - the size of the grid and the number of queries.
The second line contains a binary string of length NNN, the first row of the initial binary grid.
The third line contains a binary string of length NNN, the second row of the initial binary grid.
The next QQQ lines contain 222 integers each - XXX and YYY, representing we should flip cell (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y).



Some input lines may have extra trailing whitespaces.



Output Format
For each test case, output Q+1Q + 1Q+1 integers, the initial answer and the answer after the first iii updates for each 1≤i≤Q1 \le i \le Q1≤i≤Q. (**Since Q=0Q = 0Q=0 here, we always print 111 integer only**)
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: We can do the following 444 operations (in this order):


Choose X=1,Y=1X = 1, Y = 1X=1,Y=1 for an operation.


Choose X=1,Y=2X = 1, Y = 2X=1,Y=2 for an operation.


Choose X=2,Y=2X = 2, Y = 2X=2,Y=2 for an operation.


Choose X=2,Y=1X = 2, Y = 1X=2,Y=1 for an operation.


At the end, it can be shown that the binary grid has been transformed to:
110
011
which has the path (1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)→(2,3)(1, 1) \rightarrow (1, 2) \rightarrow (2, 2) \rightarrow (2, 3)(1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)→(2,3) going only right or down (and consisting of 111s) from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3).


Test case 2: It can be shown that it is impossible to move from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) irrespective of the operations we do.
